metadata.subject: Data Structures (CS1101)
metadata.school: Faculty of Computer Science, Alexandru Ioan Cuza University of Iasi, Romania

curricular_alignment.curriculum_area: Computer Science - Data Structures and Algorithms
general_competencies: Identify and apply fundamental data structures to solve computational problems
Analyse and compare algorithm efficiency using asymptotic notation
Design and implement algorithmic solutions in C/pseudocode
Apply ADT concepts to concrete programming problems
Evaluate trade-offs between different data structure choices
specific_competencies: Explain The implementation of digraphs with adjacency matrices
Explain The implementation with adjacency lists
Apply Finding the connected components (undirected graphs)
learning_standards: CS1101 – The implementation with adjacency matrices
CS1101 – The implementation with adjacency linked lists
key_competencies: The representation of graphs as digraphs
The implementation of digraphs with adjacency matrices

unit_overview.unit_description: Graphs are introduced as a general model for pairwise relationships. The unit covers undirected and directed graphs, adjacency-matrix and adjacency-list representations, DFS, BFS, and algorithms for connected/strongly connected components.
unit_overview.unit_rationale: Graphs are among the most powerful modelling tools in computer science, appearing in networking, logistics, social networks, and compilers.
big_ideas: A graph G=(V,E) is a flexible model for any binary relationship.
The choice of representation (matrix vs list) trades memory for access speed.
DFS and BFS are the two fundamental graph exploration strategies.
essential_questions: When is a graph a better model than a tree?
How does edge density influence which representation to choose?
What problems can be solved in a single DFS pass?
enduring_understandings: Adjacency matrix: O(V^2) space, O(1) edge lookup.
DFS runs in O(V+E) and discovers back, tree, and cross edges.
Kosaraju/Tarjan algorithm finds SCCs in O(V+E).
unit_overview.real_world_relevance: The internet is a graph. Social networks are graphs. Dependency resolution in package managers uses topological sort on a DAG.

required_concepts: Lectures 1–4: algorithms, complexity, linear data structures
required_skills: Programming in C: variables, control flow, functions, arrays, pointers
Prior units as listed in unit_position.previous_unit
common_misconceptions: Assuming DFS always gives the shortest path - that is BFS on unweighted graphs.

methods: Entry quiz at start of first lesson
criteria: Student can define: graph, vertex, edge, digraph

knowledge_outcomes: Define and explain: graph, vertex, edge, digraph, adjacency matrix
Describe the algorithmic complexity of each operation covered
State the trade-offs between implementations discussed
skill_outcomes: Implement the covered data structures and algorithms in C
Analyse given code and determine its asymptotic complexity
Select the appropriate data structure for a given problem
attitude_or_value_outcomes: Appreciate that choosing the right data structure is as important as writing correct code
Develop habits of testing and verifying algorithmic implementations
transfer_outcomes: Apply the covered data structures in subsequent units and projects
Recognise instances of the covered problems in real-world software systems

core_concepts: The representation of graphs as digraphs
The implementation of digraphs with adjacency matrices

subtopics.title: Graphs
subtopics.description: Students will understand and be able to apply the core concepts of Graphs, including: Abstract data type Graph, Abstract data type Digraph, The implementation with adjacency matrices....

lesson_sequence.focus: Lecture 7: Graphs – representations, traversals, and connected components
learning_objectives: Explain The implementation of digraphs with adjacency matrices
Explain The implementation with adjacency lists
Apply Finding the connected components (undirected graphs)
activities: Solve an exercise related to: Graphs
Solve an exercise related to: Digraph (directed graph)
assessment_evidence: Successful algorithm tracing during guided practice
lesson_sequence.homework_or_extension: Implement in C: The DFS exploration: example. Complexity: O(n log n) target where applicable.

collaborative_learning_opportunities: Group code review of C implementations
teaching_and_learning_strategy.inquiry_or_problem_based_learning: Each lesson opens with a motivating problem that the new data structure solves.
teaching_and_learning_strategy.differentiation_strategy: Simpler examples for struggling students; extension problems and complexity proofs for advanced students.

introductory_activities: Motivating problem: present a scenario where naive approaches fail
Review prerequisite concepts with a 5-minute warm-up quiz
exploration_activities: Trace algorithm execution on a small example by hand
Predict algorithm output before running it
independent_practice_activities: Solve an exercise related to: Graphs
Solve an exercise related to: Digraph (directed graph)
Solve an exercise related to: The implementation of digraphs with adjacency matrices
Solve an exercise related to: The implementation with adjacency lists
Solve an exercise related to: Digraphs: systematic exploration
collaborative_activities: Pair-trace: one student traces, the other checks
Group discussion: compare complexities of two implementations
project_or_performance_tasks: End-of-unit programming assignment: implement and benchmark the main data structure(s) of Graphs
reflection_activities: What is the key insight behind: Graphs?
What is the key insight behind: Digraph (directed graph)?
What is the key insight behind: The implementation of digraphs with adjacency matrices?

textbooks_or_references: Madalina Raschip, Cristian Gatu - CS1101 Data Structures Lecture Notes
T.H. Cormen, C.E. Leiserson, R.L. Rivest, C. Stein - Introduction to Algorithms (CLRS), 3rd ed.
D. Knuth - The Art of Computer Programming, Vol. 1-3
R. Sedgewick, K. Wayne - Algorithms, 4th ed.
manipulatives_or_physical_materials: Index cards for tracing pointer-based structures
teacher_preparation_materials: Prepare 2-3 worked examples per lesson with step-by-step solutions
Prepare extension problems for advanced students

diagnostic_assessment.purpose: Identify prerequisite gaps before starting the unit

formative_assessment.purpose: Monitor understanding during each lesson and adjust instruction
methods: Exit tickets at end of lecture
formative_assessment.feedback_strategy: Immediate verbal feedback in class; written feedback on exit tickets returned next session

summative_assessment.purpose: Evaluate mastery of unit outcomes at the end of the unit
grading_criteria: Correctness of the implemented algorithm/data structure
Appropriate use of the data structure presented in the unit
Correct complexity analysis provided in code comments
Clean, well-structured, and commented C code

methods: Self-check checklist at end of each lesson
prompts: Can I define the key concepts without notes?
Can I trace the algorithm with a new example?
Can I explain the complexity analysis?

methods: Peer code review of C implementations

observations: Teacher observation of student problem-solving strategies
student_reflections: What is the key insight behind: Graphs?
What is the key insight behind: Digraph (directed graph)?
What is the key insight behind: The implementation of digraphs with adjacency matrices?

support_for_struggling_students: Simpler worked examples with smaller inputs
Reference sheet with key definitions and complexity tables
extensions_for_advanced_students: Analyse a more complex variant of the covered algorithm
Compare the covered approach with an alternative from the literature
Prove a stated property or complexity bound rigorously
adaptations_for_special_needs: Provide printed slides before the lecture
language_support: Key vocabulary list in both English and Romanian
Bilingual lecture slides available on request
accessibility_considerations: Use high-contrast colours on the whiteboard
Provide digital versions of all handouts

classroom_management.classroom_setup: Lecture hall with projector; lab sessions in computer lab
routines: Lecture begins with a 5-minute warm-up problem on the board
Last 5 minutes reserved for exit ticket and homework assignment
behavior_expectations: Raise hand before asking a question
Keep devices on silent unless used for coding
transition_strategies: Verbal cue to signal end of note-taking and start of exercise mode
2-minute stretch break between content presentation and guided practice
technology_management: Coding exercises completed in designated lab sessions
Online resources permitted only for approved reference sites
risk_points: Complexity analysis can become abstract - always pair with a concrete example
Students may struggle with pointer-based structures - allocate extra time for visualisation
backup_plans: If projector fails, deliver lecture using whiteboard with pre-written notes
If lab computers are unavailable, complete coding exercises as take-home work

weekly_breakdown.focus: Lecture 7: Graphs – representations, traversals, and connected components
assessments: Exit ticket: one short question about a key concept

project_links: End-of-unit project: apply Graphs to a real-world problem

homework_tasks: Implement in C: The DFS exploration: example. Complexity: O(n log n) target where applicable.
optional_practice: Solve additional exercises from CLRS relevant to Graphs
Experiment with edge cases in your C implementation
extension_tasks: Research an advanced variant of the covered data structure
Read and summarise a Wikipedia article on an application of the unit topic
family_or_home_connections: Identify a real-world system that uses the data structure studied in this unit

review_activities: In-class Q&A review of key concepts
Kahoot-style quiz covering all unit vocabulary
synthesis_tasks: Core concept covered: The implementation of digraphs with adjacency matrices
Core concept covered: The implementation with adjacency lists
Core concept covered: Digraphs: systematic exploration
final_reflection_prompts: What was the most surprising thing you learned in Unit 5: Graphs?
Which concept took the longest to understand and why?
How would you explain the main data structure of this unit to a friend?
unit_closure.student_showcase_or_presentation: Optional: students present their end-of-unit programming project to the class
unit_closure.transition_to_next_unit: Preview of Unit 6: Sorting Algorithms: how does it build on what we have learned?
